title: Wooden Gear Wall Clock
summary: A wall clock shaped like a mechanical gear, cut from a single board with a scroll saw and finished with a standard quartz movement.
description:
This clock gets its entire visual identity from one cut — the gear-tooth silhouette — rather than from an elaborate mechanism. A standard off-the-shelf quartz clock movement does all the actual timekeeping; the woodworking is entirely in the shape.

The **board needs to be at least 1/4" thick to accept the clock mechanism's shaft**, which is the one hard constraint on material choice. Beyond that, any board wide enough for a roughly 12" circle works.

A printed gear template, taped or spray-adhesived to the board, replaces a numeric cut list entirely here — trace the template rather than trying to reconstruct gear-tooth geometry from measurements.

tools.note: Or a scroll saw for tighter tooth detail.
Drills the center hole sized to the clock mechanism's shaft.

materials.name: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Transfer the gear template
steps.body: Print a gear template and tape or spray-adhesive it onto the board.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Cut the gear shape
steps.body: Cut out the gear silhouette with a jigsaw, following the template closely around each tooth.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Drill the center hole
steps.body: Find the center of the blank and drill a hole sized to the clock mechanism's shaft with a forstner bit.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the cut edges and both faces smooth, then paint or stain as desired.

steps.title: Install the mechanism
steps.body: Install the clock mechanism through the center hole and set the hands.
